para.text: Numbered Seals Required. Any seal used by a local board shall be numbered.
Reception Team to Receive. The sealed ballot boxes from the polling places shall be received by a reception team.
Marking.
Each ballot box shall be marked as having been inspected before it is delivered to a ballot reception team; and
Each ballot box shall be marked with the batch label prior to certification in §D of this regulation.
Inspection and Certification.
The reception team shall:
Inspect each ballot box and its seal; and
Prepare and sign a certification of the box’s condition.
The certification for each box shall include:
The precinct number;
The seal number;
The condition of seal;
Any defects observed; and
The batch label.

annotations.annotation: Election Law Article, §§2-102(b)(4), 2-202(b), and 11-309, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: February 29, 2016 (43:3 Md. R. 334)
Regulation .01 amended effective April 27, 2026 (53:8 Md. R. 356)

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Automated software audit” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §11-309, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Ballot-level comparison audit” means a type of risk-limiting audit in which the local board examines voter selections on randomly selected individual paper ballots and compares them with corresponding cast vote records.
“Ballot polling audit” means a type of risk-limiting audit in which the local board examines voter selections on randomly selected ballots.

“Ballot manifest” means a detailed catalog prepared by the local board listing the batch label, number of ballots, and any other material required by the State Board as necessary to identify ballots for the risk-limiting audit.
“Batch” means a grouping of ballots by criteria determined by the State Board and individually stored in a ballot box.
“Batch comparison audit” means a type of risk-limiting audit in which the local board examines voter markings on randomly selected batches of ballots against the voting system counts.
“Batch label” means the uniform naming convention determined by the State Board that is used to identify the batch of ballots.
“Cast vote records” means an auditable document or electronic record that purports to reflect the selections a voter made on a ballot.
“Discrepancy” means the difference between the voting system results and the results of an automated or risk-limiting audit.
“Manual count” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §11-309, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Precinct” includes an early voting center in Regulations .02—.06 of this chapter.
“Random seed” means a randomly generated number that is used to generate a random number sequence to select ballot cards or batches of ballot cards for audit.
“Risk limit” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §11-309, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Risk-limiting audit” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §11-309, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Risk-limiting audit software” means the software used by the State Board in order to conduct the risk-limiting audit.
“Voter-verifiable paper record” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §9-102, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Automatic.
For every precinct in an election, the State Administrator shall produce a report with the following information:
The number of voters who were checked-in to vote as reported by the electronic pollbooks;
The number of ballots cast as reported in the Election Management System; and
The difference, if any, between the number of voters checked-in to vote and ballots cast.

The election director shall conduct the audit procedures under §B of this regulation for all batches of ballots scanned at that precinct if the State Administrator’s report indicates that the difference between the number of voters checked-in to vote and the number of ballots cast is five or more.
Automatic Audit Procedures—Addition of Batches to Risk-Limiting Audit.
Batches shall be added to risk-limiting audit under COMAR 33.08.05.09. The election director shall:
Add the batches identified in §A of this regulation to the batches audited by the risk limiting audit; and
Conduct the audit procedures under this section for each batch of ballots cast at precinct identified under §A of this regulation.
For each batch being audited, the election director shall also:
Hand count the signed voter authority cards associated with each voting unit used to scan the batch; and
Compare the number of signed voter authority cards against the number of ballots cast as reported in the Election Management System.
If the number of signed voter authority cards does not match the number of ballots cast, the election director shall:
Review other election materials from the precinct to determine the cause of the difference; and
Report to the State Administrator the explanation for the difference.
Notification. The election director shall report to the local board and the State Administrator the results of the audit.

section.heading: Post-Election Audit — Ballot Tabulation Audit — In General.

para.text: Audits Conducted by the State Administrator. The State Administrator shall conduct an automated software audit of the electronic images of all ballots cast:
After each Statewide primary election; and
After each Statewide general election.
Audit Conducted by the Local Boards. Each local board shall conduct a risk-limiting audit of voter-verifiable paper records after each Statewide election:
Reporting of Audit Results.
The State Administrator shall post on the State Board website:
Prior to certification of the election by the Board of State Canvassers, the results of the risk-limiting audit, including:
An overview of the risk-limiting audit process; and
An explanation or video of the batch selection process; and
Within 1 week after certification of election results by the Board of State Canvassers, the results of the automated software audit, including:
An overview of the automated audit process;
The audit reports generated for each county;
An explanation of any discrepancy greater than 0.5 percent of total votes cast in any given contest; and
Any additional steps taken to resolve any discrepancy.

Prior to certification of the election by the Board of State Canvassers, the State Administrator shall report the results of the risk-limiting audit to the Board of State Canvassers and the State Board.

section.heading: Post-Election Audit — Ballot Tabulation Audit — Risk Limiting Audit.

para.text: In General.
Audit Method.
Except as provided in §A(1)(b) of this regulation, a batch comparison audit shall be conducted in accordance with Election Law Article, §11-309, Annotated Code of Maryland.

Exception to Batch Comparison. If the State Administrator determines that a batch comparison audit cannot be completed prior to the Board of State Canvassers certification, the audit may be conducted using the following methods:
Ballot comparison;
Ballot polling; or
A hybrid of batch comparison, ballot comparison, or ballot polling.
Risk Limit. The risk limit shall be 10 percent.
Timing of Audit.
No later than 17 days after election day, the local board shall provide to the State Board a ballot manifest, in the manner prescribed by the State Board.
The local board shall commence a risk-limiting audit no sooner than 21 days after election day.
The local board shall complete a risk-limiting audit no later than 2 days before the Board of State Canvassers convenes under Election Law Article, §11-503(a)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland.
Public Notice and Observation.
A local board shall:
At least 3 days before the risk-limiting audit starts, provide notice of the risk-limiting audit by:
Sending the notice to the chairman of the county central committee, each candidate for the contest to be audited who is not a candidate of a principal political party, and the State Administrator;
Posting the notice on its website; and
Posting the notice in a prominent and publicly accessible location at its office; and
Allow, to the extent practicable, for public observation of each part of the manual count of ballots of the risk-limiting audit process.
The State Board shall:
Provide at least 3 days notice of the time, date, and location for conducting risk-limiting audit initiation procedures required in §E of this regulation by posting the notice on its website; and
Allow, to the extent practicable, for public observation of risk-limiting audit initiation procedures.
Selection of Contests for Audit.
Determination of Number of Statewide Contests. No later than the day before the start of the risk-limiting audit, the State Board shall determine the number of contests to be audited as follows:
Consider the number of Statewide contests in the election;
Consider local boards’ resources, including provisional processing, canvass, and recounts;
For a primary election:
If one contest is selected, require auditing of at least one Statewide contest on one party’s ballot;
If two contests are selected, require auditing of at least one Statewide contest on each party’s ballot; and
In a general election, require auditing of at least one Statewide contest.

Determination of Statewide Contest. No later than the day before the start of the risk-limiting audit, the State Board shall randomly select from all Statewide contests at least one contest to be audited.
Requests by Local Boards to Audit Local Contests.
A local board may conduct a risk-limiting audit of a local contest at the discretion of the local board.
If a local board conducts a risk-limiting audit of a local contest:
All Statewide contests must be completed prior to the commencement of the audit; and
The timing of completion of the risk-limiting audit of the Statewide contest may not be affected by the local contest audit.
A local board may request the State Administrator’s approval in writing no later than 1 month prior to the election, to use the risk-limiting audit software to conduct the local contest audit.
Local Board Preparations for Audit.
Ballot Control. Each local board shall store ballots as follow:
In a sealed ballot box, with only ballots from the same batch; and
With the batch label on the exterior of the ballot box, which identifies the batch as it appears on the ballot manifest.
Requirement to Provide Ballot Manifest. Each local board shall provide to the State Board no later than 17 days after election day:
A ballot manifest in the manner prescribed by the State Board;
An estimate of all provisional and mail-in ballots that have not yet been canvassed; and
Certification that all batches listed on the ballot manifest have been verified by the local board as required by the State Board.
Initiation of Risk-Limiting Audit. On the day before the first day of the risk limiting audit, the State Administrator shall hold a public meeting during which the State Administrator shall:
Select random choice of a seed number to input into risk-limiting audit software; and
Using the randomly-selected seed, risk limit, and margin of victory for the contest or contests selected to be audited, determine the ballots to be audited by each local board.
Manual Count Procedures.
Each election director shall:
Prepare for a risk-limiting audit by recruiting and training manual count teams;
Instruct teams to use the sort method described in §F(2) of this regulation if one Statewide contest are audited;
Instruct teams to use the tally method described in §F(3) of this regulation if more than one Statewide or local contest is audited;
Issue the teams batches or voter verifiable paper records chosen by the risk-limiting software for manual count; and
Record in the audit log:
The team identifier;
The voter verifiable paper records issued to the team; and
Upon completion, the voter verifiable paper records returned by the team;
Enter the vote totals reported by manual count teams on the consolidated tally sheets;
Compare the results of the manual count against the voting system results for those voter verifiable paper records;
If there are any inaccuracies, retabulate the voter verifiable paper records;
If manual and electronic counts agree, complete and sign the tally sheet; and
Within 24 hours after completion of the audit, submit the results to the State Administrator.
Sort Method. Each manual count team shall:
Designate one team member who shall sort and a second team member who shall watch to ensure accuracy;
Sort all assigned voter verifiable paper records into one of the following batches:
A batch for each candidate or ballot question response for a contest being audited;
A batch for voter verifiable paper records without a vote for a contest being audited;
A batch for each officially filed write-in candidate for a contest being audited; and
A batch for all other write-in votes;
If team members do not agree on how a vote should be counted:
Refer the voter verifiable paper record to the election director; and
For auditing purposes only, accept the election director’s determination of how the vote shall be counted;
Once all of the voter verifiable paper records have been sorted, ensure that each team member independently counts the voter verifiable paper records in each batch;
If the team members’ results are not identical, retabulate the voter verifiable paper records until obtaining identical results; and
When the team members’ results are identical:
Record the vote totals on the batch tally sheet;
Sign the batch tally sheet; and
Give the batch tally sheet and the voter verifiable paper records to the election director.
Tally Method. Each manual count team shall:
Include one caller, one watcher, and two tally clerks;
For each voter verifiable paper record assigned to it:
Have its caller call the votes cast in the contests being audited;
Have its watcher ensure the accuracy of the calling; and
Have its two tally clerks independently record the votes as they are called;
Ensure that its tally clerks periodically compare their results;

If the tally clerks’ results are not identical, retabulate voter verifiable paper records beginning with the point of the last successful comparison check, and repeat until the two tally clerks obtain identical results; and
When all votes assigned to the team have been tallied, ensure that its tally clerks:
Record the vote totals on the batch tally sheet;
Sign the batch tally sheet; and
Give the batch tally sheet and the voter verifiable paper records to the election director.
Resolving Electronic Count.

If the manual count of results in an audited contest does not confirm the electronic count of results for that contest, the local board of canvassers shall investigate the matter to determine an accurate result, including:
A review of all tally sheets and other documentary materials from the risk-limiting audit;
A review of all canvass minutes;
Interviews with the election director; and
Any other information that would explain the difference between the audit and the electronic count of results.
No later than 3 days after beginning the investigation, the local board of canvassers shall:
Provide to the State Administrator written findings explaining the difference; and
If it concludes that the electronic count is inaccurate, adopt corrections to certified results in accordance with Election Law, §§11-308 and 11-401, Annotated Code Maryland.

para.text: Required Information — In General. The technical data package shall contain all documentation previously submitted for the qualification testing of the system, including each of the following:
Hardware schematic diagrams;
Hardware theory of operation;
Customer maintenance documentation;
Operations manual;
Software source code (both in the form of a listing and in a machine-readable form on media acceptable to the evaluation agent);
Software system design;
Customer documentation;
Any known and unresolved system problems;
When available, the report and test results of the independent testing laboratory; and
Any capability for the system to conduct a risk limiting audit of its tabulated results.
Required Information — License and Warranty. The package also shall include:
A list of all third-party software required to install or operate the system; and
The terms of the license agreements for all software identified under §B(1) of this regulation.
Identification of Submissions. Each item in the package shall be clearly identified.

para.text: After completing canvassing procedures, a local board shall retain all voted ballots in a sealed ballot box labeled with a batch label until they are authorized to be opened and removed for an audit, recount, judicial challenge, storage, or disposal.
Document Retention. A local board shall retain for 22 months after an election:
Absentee ballot applications;
Voted absentee ballots;
Return envelopes; and
Certificates of assistance.
Disposal of Unvoted Ballots.
A local board may dispose of any unvoted absentee ballots at least 30 days after State certification of an election or the conclusion of a recount, whichever is later.
A local board is not required to shred unvoted absentee ballots.

para.text: In accordance with COMAR 33.16, the local board may process provisional ballot applications received during the early voting period.
The local board shall maintain ballots voted during early voting:
Grouped by batch number; and
Separate from ballots voted on election day, absentee ballots, and provisional ballots.

para.text: State Board. After the close of registration for each election, but prior to election day, the State Administrator shall take appropriate measures to notify each pre-qualified voter how to find the individual’s correct polling place.
Local Board. A local board shall include in each specimen ballot information about how a voter can change the voter’s address during early voting.

para.text: A facility operator shall install in its facility a surveillance system that complies with the requirements of this regulation.
A facility operator’s surveillance system shall be reviewed and approved by the Commission under Regulation .12 of this chapter.
A facility operator’s surveillance system shall include:
Light sensitive cameras enabled by:
Lenses of sufficient magnification to read a video lottery terminal reel strip and credit meter;
Lighting which is continuous and of sufficient quality to produce clear video recordings and still pictures; and
360-degree pan, tilt, and zoom capability, without camera stops, configured to clandestinely monitor and record:
Play and transactions conducted at video lottery terminals;
Transactions conducted in the cashiers' cage and any satellite cage including the face of each individual transacting business with a cashier;
Transactions conducted at ticket redemption units, automated jackpot payout machines, and automated teller machines;
Activity in the count room;
Movement of cash and cash storage boxes within the facility;
Entrances and exits to the facility and the gaming floor;
Activities in all other restricted areas; and
Other areas and events designated by the Commission;
A monitor room located in the facility:
Staffed by employees of the facility operator’s surveillance department 24 hours per day; and
Equipped with:
A communication system capable of monitoring all security department communications;
Connections, direct or through a documented communication protocol with the security department, to all facility alarm systems;
A surveillance failure notification system that provides an audible, as well as a visual notification, of any failure in the surveillance system or the digital video recording media storage system;

An emergency power system, tested by the facility operator in the presence of the Commission at least once a year, which can be used to operate the surveillance system in the event of a power failure;
Computer terminals permitting event notification to, and read only access by authorized surveillance department employees to, the facility operator’s video lottery system;
An updated photo library, consisting of photographs that are no more than 5 years old, of all current employees of the facility;
A copy of the facility operator’s gaming floor plan required under COMAR 36.03.11.05;
A copy of the procedures addressing the evacuation of the facility in the event of fire or other emergency required under Regulation .14 of this chapter; and
Copies of the surveillance system contingency plans required under Regulation .12 of this chapter;
Digital video recording capability equipped to:
Superimpose the date and time on all monitoring and recording;
Identify and locate, through the use of a meter, counter, or other device or method, a particular event which was recorded;
Identify on video recording disks or other storage media the type of media player and software prerequisite to viewing the digital images; and
Be authenticated through use of an embedded video verification encryption code or watermark;
Audio recording capability in the count room that is:
Installed and disclosed to employees of the facility; and
Consistent with Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, §§10-401—10-414, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
An access system which:
Controls:
Physical and logical access to the surveillance system; and
Physical access to the surveillance monitor room; and
Restricts access to the security administration capabilities of the system.
A facility operator shall configure its surveillance system to record all areas and transactions enumerated in §C(1)(c) of this regulation with a resolution of 4 common image format at a minimum of:
For a standard definition camera, 30 frames per second; or
For a high definition camera, 15 frames per second.

A facility operator may configure its surveillance system to record activity in areas of the facility not covered by §D of this regulation at a reduced frame rate with a resolution of 4 common image format, as follows:
Public areas shall be recorded at a minimum frame rate of 15 frames per second; and
Areas not accessible to the public shall be recorded at a minimum frame rate of 7.5 frames per second.
Except as provided in §G of this regulation, a facility operator shall retain surveillance recordings:
For a minimum of 7 days for transactions or events in the areas covered under §E of this regulation; and
For a minimum of 14 days for transactions or event in the areas covered under §D of this regulation.

Upon the request of the Commission or a law enforcement agency that has proper jurisdiction over the facility, a recording shall be retained and stored in accordance with the directives of the Commission or law enforcement agency pertaining to that recording.
Except as provided in this regulation, the surveillance system shall be under the exclusive control of the facility operator’s surveillance department.
A facility operator shall provide the Commission with timely and unfettered access to its surveillance monitor room, surveillance system, and all transmissions.
A facility operator shall timely comply with a request from the Commission to:
Use, as necessary, any monitor room in the facility;
Display on the monitors in its monitor room or in the Commission’s on-site monitor room any event capable of being captured by the surveillance system;
Relinquish control of a camera or monitor;
Discontinue monitoring a particular camera or recording activity captured by it;
Make a video recording or photograph of any event capable of being captured by the surveillance system; and provide the recording:
In a compact disc;
Using a secure drive; or
Another method approved by the Commission; and
Restrict or deny access to a recording or photograph.
A surveillance system may not be remotely accessed from a location outside the surveillance monitor room without the prior written approval of the Commission.
An entrance to a surveillance monitor room may not be visible from the gaming floor.
At least 60 days before video lottery terminal or table game operations are to commence, a facility operator shall submit to the Commission for review and written approval:
A surveillance system meeting the requirements of Regulation .11 of this chapter including, at a minimum, details pertaining to:
Camera configuration inside and outside the facility;
Monitor room configuration;
Video recording format and configuration specifications;
Authentication of digital recordings, including Commission access to the system’s video verification encryption code or watermark;
Audio recording format; and
System access controls; and
Surveillance department operating procedures conforming to this regulation.
A facility operator may not commence operations until its surveillance system and surveillance department operating procedures are approved in writing by the Commission.
A facility operator’s surveillance department operating procedures shall, at a minimum, require:
Coverage of all areas and transactions enumerated in Regulation .11 of this chapter;
Contingency plans addressing:
Full and partial failure of the surveillance system including:
A contact list with telephone numbers for individuals required to be notified in the event of a failure; and
Facility closure protocols; or
An electronic format equipped with software that documents changes made by each user; and
Planned shutdown of the surveillance system;
A surveillance incident log:
Maintained by monitor room employees in:
A book with bound numbered pages that cannot be readily removed;
An electronic format equipped with software that prevents modification of an entry after it has been initially entered into the system; or
An electronic format equipped with software that documents changes made by each user; and
Documenting the scheduled coverage in §C(1) of this regulation and all other nonroutine surveillance activity as follows:
Date and time surveillance is commenced;
Name and Commission license number of the individual initiating, performing, or supervising the surveillance;
Reason for the surveillance;
Whether the suspicious activity involves an alleged regulatory violation or criminal activity;
Name, if known, alias, or description of an individual being monitored;
Description of the activity in which the individual being monitored is engaged;
Reading on a meter, counter, or device that identifies the point on the video recording at which the event was recorded;
Time at which a video recording is commenced and terminated, if different than when surveillance is commenced or terminated;
Date and time surveillance is terminated;
Summary of the results of the surveillance; and
Description of the time, date, and cause of any equipment or camera malfunction which occurred during the conduct of surveillance;
A surveillance room entry log:
To be signed by an individual entering the surveillance monitor room who is not a surveillance department employee assigned to the monitor room’s work shift at the time of entry;
Maintained by monitor room employees in:
A book with bound numbered pages that cannot be readily removed; or
An electronic format equipped with software that prevents modification of an entry after it has been initially entered into the system; and
Documenting the following:
Date and time of entering the monitor room;
The entering individual's name and department or affiliation;
The reason for entering the monitor room;
The name of the individual authorizing the individual's entry into the monitor room; and
The date and time of exiting the monitor room;
That surveillance monitor room employees notify:
Security department supervisory personnel within 5 minutes of an incident of equipment failure affecting coverage of the facility; and
The Commission within 30 minutes of an incident of equipment failure affecting coverage of the facility citing:
Date and time;
Cause of the malfunction; and
Time the facility operator’s security department was notified of the malfunction;
That the facility operator confirm in writing a notice given verbally to the Commission under §C(5) of this regulation; and
That, on a daily basis, the facility operator synchronize the date and time on the surveillance system to the date and time on the central monitor and control system and its video lottery system.

A facility operator may not implement a change or amendment in its surveillance system or surveillance department operating procedures approved by the Commission under §B of this regulation without the prior written approval of the Commission.
Surveillance department employees shall be reasonably segregated and independent of all other departments at the facility.
A surveillance department employee may not transfer to any other department in the facility without the prior written approval of the Commission.

section.heading: Collection of Cash Storage and Table Game Drop Boxes.

para.text: At least 30 days before video lottery terminal or table game operations are to commence, a facility operator shall submit to the Commission in writing a drop schedule setting forth:
Specific pick-up days and times for collection of cash storage and table game drop boxes and requirements that:
Cash storage boxes may not be commingled with table game drop boxes;
Unless a drop box is from a table game pit that was never opened for gaming on that gaming day, table game drop boxes shall be collected once each gaming day; and
The facility operator notify the Commission by telephone and in writing 1 hour in advance of changes to the table game drop box collection schedule required under §A(1)(b) of this regulation.
Specifications as to what areas of the gaming floor will be covered on each pick-up day; and
Specific transportation routes to be utilized from the gaming floor to the count room on each pick-up day.
A facility operator shall notify the Commission:
In writing, of a permanent change in the drop schedule including a pick-up day or time, area of the floor to be dropped, or transportation route; and
Prior to any temporary deviation from the drop schedule.
A facility operator shall make readily available to the Commission:
An access control matrix indicating which employee job descriptions are authorized to participate in the cash storage and table game drop box collection process; and
A list of employees, with license numbers, who are authorized to participate in the cash storage and table game drop box collection process.

A facility operator shall develop and include in the internal controls submitted to and approved by the Commission under Regulation .05 of this chapter procedures addressing the collection of cash storage and table game drop boxes.
A facility operator’s internal controls shall:
Detail the actual procedures to be performed and documentation to be generated by drop team employees collecting cash storage and table game drop boxes:
In accordance with the drop schedule; and
On an emergency basis; and
Require:
Cash storage and table game drop boxes to be transported directly to, and secured in:
The count room; or
A trolley storage area approved by the Commission under COMAR 36.03.11;
The cash storage and table game drop box collection process to involve the participation of at least three employees, at least one of whom is an employee of the:
Security department; and
Accounting department;

Prior to the movement of a trolley containing cash storage boxes from the gaming floor into the count room, an accounting department supervisor to verify that the number of cash storage boxes being transported from the gaming floor equals the number of cash storage boxes scheduled for collection that day;

Prior to the movement of a trolley containing table game drop boxes from the gaming floor into the count room, an accounting department supervisor or floorperson or above to verify that the number of table game drop boxes being transported from the gaming floor equals the number of table game drop boxes scheduled for collection; and
Prior to changing the type of table game offered, or removing a video lottery terminal or table game from the gaming floor, that an emergency drop shall be conducted.
A facility operator shall transport cash storage and table game drop boxes in an enclosed trolley secured by one lock that has a key which is controlled by the security department.
A facility operator shall store cash storage and table game drop boxes not attached to a bill validator, including emergency cash storage and table game drop boxes that are not actively in use:
In the count room in an area approved by the Commission;
In a trolley storage area approved by the Commission under COMAR 36.03.11; or
In another location at the facility approved by the Commission.
The cabinet or trolley used for storage under §G of this regulation shall be secured by one lock that has a key which is controlled by the security department in:
A manual key box; or
An automated key tracking system.

Immediately prior to the commencement of the count process, the security department may issue its key to the storage cabinet or trolley required under §G of this regulation to a count room supervisor for the purpose of allowing count room personnel to gain access to the cash storage or table game drop boxes to be counted.
A trolley storage area utilized to store cash storage or table game drop boxes prior to the count process shall meet the design standards for a count room under Regulation .44 of this chapter.
Except for security officers, the drop and count team members assigned to the collection of cash storage or table game drop boxes shall wear a one-piece, pocketless jumpsuit.

para.text: Surveillance System. In addition to the surveillance system requirements in Regulation .03 of this chapter and COMAR 36.03.10, a facility operator that offers table games shall have a surveillance system that includes:
Light sensitive cameras with lenses of sufficient magnification to allow the certificate holder to clandestinely monitor in detail:
The gaming conducted at each gaming table in the licensed facility with sufficient clarity and coverage to simultaneously:
Identify patrons and dealers; and
View the table and determine the configuration of wagers, card, dice and tile values and game outcomes;
The movement of cash, gaming chips, and plaques, tip boxes, and drop boxes within the facility; and
Any other activity or areas designated by the Commission; and
Stationary cameras dedicated to each table game offered by the facility, which shall be:
Except for Craps, Baccarat, Roulette and Big Six Wheel, for each table game, at least one stationary camera;
For a Craps table, at least two stationary cameras, with one camera covering each end of the table;
For a Baccarat table:
That is 72 inches or longer, at least two stationary cameras, with one camera covering each end of the table; or
That is less than 72 inches, at least one stationary camera covering the table;
For a Roulette table, at least two stationary cameras, with one camera covering the Roulette wheel and one camera covering the Roulette table layout;
For a Big Six Wheel, at least two stationary cameras, with one camera covering the Big Six Wheel and one camera covering the Big Six Wheel table layout;
Additional cameras as required by the Commission, which may include cameras with 360-degree pan, tilt, and zoom capabilities; and
Single stationary cameras that:
Are capable of clearly identifying the entire table layout, conduct, and outcome of the game; and
May be used by a facility operator in lieu of cameras identified in §A(2)(b)—(e) of this regulation.
Required Recordings. A facility’s surveillance system must continuously record transmissions from cameras used to observe the:
Gaming conducted at table games;
Collection of drop boxes and tip boxes;
Distribution of cards, dice, and tiles to gaming pits;
Inspection of cards, dice, and tiles in the gaming pits and at the gaming tables;
Retrieval of cards, dice, and tiles from the gaming pits at the end of the gaming day; and
Delivery of cards, dice, and tiles to the location designated and approved by the Commission for inspection, cancellation, destruction, or, if applicable, packaging for reuse.
Retention.
The surveillance recordings required under §B of this regulation shall be retained for a minimum of 14 days.
A surveillance recording of suspicious activity, suspected or alleged regulatory violations, or suspected or alleged criminal activity shall be retained for a minimum of 30 days.
A surveillance recording shall be made available for review upon request by law enforcement.
Commission approval of monitoring rooms.
Prior to the commencement of table game operations, a facility shall submit to the Commission a revised minimum staffing submission for the facility operator’s surveillance monitor rooms.

The minimum staffing submission must consider the size and layout of the licensed facility as well as the number of table games and must at all times provide for surveillance of activities inside and outside the licensed facility.
A facility operator may not implement a surveillance plan, or an amendment to a surveillance plan or minimum staffing submission, without prior Commission approval.

section.heading: Procedure for Distributing Chips, Coins, and Plaques to a Gaming Table.

para.text: A pit clerk or floorperson or above shall prepare a request for a fill to add value chips, coins, and plaques to a table game using a Fill Request Slip.
Access to the blank Fill Request Slips shall be restricted to pit clerks and floorpersons or above.
A Fill Request Slip shall be a two-part form on which the following information shall be recorded:
The date and time of preparation;
The denomination of value chips, coins and plaques to be distributed to the gaming table;
The total amount of each denomination of value chips, coins and plaques to be distributed to the gaming table;
The game and table number to which the value chips, coins and plaques are to be distributed; and
The signature of the floorperson or above requesting the fill.
After the Fill Request Slip has been prepared, the security department employee shall transport the chip bank copy of the Fill Request Slip directly to the chip bank.

The dealer or boxperson shall place the drop box copy of the Fill Request Slip in view of the facility operator’s surveillance system on the gaming table to which the value chips, coins, and plaques are to be received.
Notwithstanding the requirements of §§A—E of this regulation, a fill request may be prepared electronically if:
The input data for preparation of the fill is entered by, and ability to input data is restricted to, the pit clerk or a floorperson or above; and
A Fill Slip is generated in the chip bank as a direct result of the input.

A Fill Slip shall be prepared by a chip bank cashier or, if the required information was inputted in conformity with §F of this regulation, the Fill Slip may be electronically generated in the chip bank.
A Fill Slip shall be:
A serially prenumbered form;
Used in sequential order;
Accounted for by employees with no incompatible functions; and
If applicable, marked “Void” and signed by the preparer.
If a Fill Slip is manually prepared, the following procedures and requirements shall be observed:

Each series of Fill Slips shall be a four-part form and shall be inserted in a locked dispenser or bound in a Fill Slip form book that permits an individual Fill Slip in the series and its copies to be written upon while still locked in the dispenser or bound in the Fill Slip form book;
The Fill Slip dispenser shall discharge the drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Fill Slip while the accounting copy remains in a continuous, unbroken form in the dispenser; or
If a Fill Slip form book is utilized, the accounting copy must remain in the bound Fill Slip form book until removed in accordance with §I(4) of this regulation; and

Access to copies of the Fill Slips shall be maintained and controlled by accounting department employees with no incompatible functions who are responsible for controlling and accounting for the unused supply of Fill Slips, placing Fill Slips in the dispensers and removing the accounting copies of the Fill Slips from the dispensers or Fill Slip form book each gaming day.
If a Fill Slip is electronically prepared, each series of Fill Slips must be a three-part form and:
Be inserted in a printer that will simultaneously print a drop box, acknowledgment and chip bank copy of the Fill Slip in the chip bank; and

Store, in machine readable form, the information printed on the drop box, acknowledgement, and chip bank copies of the Fill Slips so that the stored data may not be susceptible to change or removal by any personnel involved in the preparation of a Fill Slip after the Fill Slip has been prepared.
A copy of a Fill Slip and, if applicable, the stored data, must contain at least the:
Denominations of the value chips, coins, and plaques being distributed;
Total amount of each denomination of value chips, coins, and plaques being distributed;
Total amount of all denominations of value chips, coins, and plaques being distributed;
Game and table number to which the value chips, coins, and plaques are being distributed;
Date and shift during which the distribution of value chips, coins, and plaques occurs; and
Signature of the preparer or, if electronically prepared, the license number of the preparer.
The time of preparation of the Fill Slip shall be recorded on the drop box, acknowledgement, and chip bank copies of the Fill Slip upon preparation.
A security department employee shall directly transport a value chip, coin or plaque distributed to a gaming table from the chip bank to a gaming table.
Upon receipt of a value chip, coin or plaque at a gaming table, the floorperson shall:
Compare the Fill Request Slip to the Fill Slip; and
Attest to the accuracy of the fill by signing the drop box copy and acknowledgement copy of the Fill Slip.
If a fill request is generated electronically in the chip bank in accordance with §F of this regulation, the floorperson shall:
Compare the Fill Slip with the electronically generated fill request; and
Attest to the accuracy of the fill by signing the drop box and acknowledgement copies of the Fill Slip.

Signatures on the drop box and acknowledgement copies of the Fill Slip attesting to the accuracy of the information contained on a Fill Slip shall be required of the specified employees at the specified times:
The chip bank cashier, upon preparation;
The security department employee transporting the value chips, coins and plaques to the gaming table, upon receipt from the cashier of the value chips, coins and plaques to be transported;
The dealer or boxperson assigned to the gaming table, upon receipt and verification of the amounts of the value chips, coins and plaques at the gaming table from the security department employee; and
The floorperson assigned to the gaming table, upon receipt and verification of the amounts of the value chips, coins and plaques at the gaming table.

After the signature requirements in §P of this regulation have been satisfied, the security department employee who transported to a gaming table a value chip, coin or plaque, the drop box, and acknowledgement copies of the Fill Slip, shall:

Observe the dealer’s or boxperson’s immediate placement of the drop box copy of the Fill Slip and the drop box copy of the Fill Request Slip, if applicable, in the drop box attached to the gaming table to which a value chip, coin, or plaque was transported; and
Return the acknowledgement copy of the Fill Slip to the chip bank cashier.

The chip bank cashier shall maintain together the chip bank copies of the Fill Request Slip, if applicable, and the chip bank and acknowledgement copies of the Fill Slip, until those items are forwarded to the accounting department.

All parts of voided Fill Slips, as well as the chip bank copies of Fill Request Slips, if applicable, and the acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Fill Slips that are maintained and controlled in conformity with §Q of this regulation, shall be forwarded to the accounting department for agreement, on a daily basis, with:
The drop box copies of the Fill Request Slips, if applicable, and Fill Slips removed from the drop box on the gaming table; and
If applicable, the electronically stored data and accounting copies of the Fill Slips.

section.heading: Removing a Value Chip, Coin, or Plaque from a Gaming Table.

para.text: A pit clerk or floorperson or above shall prepare a request for a credit to remove a value chip, coin or plaque from a table game by using a Credit Request Slip.
Access to the blank Credit Request Slips shall be restricted to pit clerks and floorpersons or above.
A Credit Request Slip shall be a two-part form on which the following information shall be recorded:
The date and time of preparation;
The denomination of chips, coins, and plaques to be removed from the gaming table;
The total amount of each denomination of value chips, coins and plaques to be removed from the gaming table;
The game and table number from which the value chips, coins, and plaques are to be removed; and
The signature of the floorperson or above assigned to the gaming table from which the value chips, coins and plaques are to be removed.
A security department employee shall transport the chip bank copy of a prepared Credit Request Slip directly to the chip bank.

A dealer or boxperson shall place the drop box copy of the Credit Request Slip in view of the facility operator’s surveillance system on the gaming table from which the value chips, coins and plaques are to be removed.
The drop box copy of the Credit Request Slip may not be removed until the drop box and acknowledgement copies of the Credit Slip are received from the chip bank.
Notwithstanding the requirements of §§A—F of this regulation, a request for a credit may be prepared electronically if:
The input data for preparation of the credit is entered by, and ability to input data is restricted to, the pit clerk or a floorperson or above; and
A Credit Slip is generated in the chip bank as a direct result of the input.

A Credit Slip shall be prepared by a chip bank cashier or, if the required information was inputted in conformity with §G of this regulation, the Credit Slip may be electronically generated in the chip bank.
Credit Slips shall be:
Serially prenumbered forms;
Used in sequential order;
Accounted for by employees with no incompatible functions; and
If applicable, marked “Void” and signed by the preparer.
When Credit Slips are manually prepared, the following procedures and requirements shall be observed:

Each series of Credit Slips shall be a four-part form and shall be inserted in a locked dispenser or bound in a Credit Slip form book that permits an individual Credit Slip in the series and its copies to be written upon while still locked in the dispenser or bound in the Credit Slip form book;
The Credit Slip dispenser must discharge the drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip while the accounting copy remains in a continuous, unbroken form in the dispenser; or
If a Credit Slip form book is utilized, the accounting copy must remain in the bound Credit Slip form book until removed in accordance with §J(4) of this regulation; and

Access to the copies of the Credit Slips shall be maintained and controlled by accounting department employees with no incompatible functions who shall be responsible for controlling and accounting for the unused supply of the Credit Slips, placing Credit Slips in the dispensers, and removing the accounting copies of the Credit Slips from the dispensers or Credit Slip form book each gaming day.
When Credit Slips are electronically prepared, each series of Credit Slips must be a three-part form and:
Be inserted in a printer that simultaneously prints drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip in the chip bank;
Store, in machine-readable form, the information printed on the drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip; and
The stored data may not be susceptible to change or removal by any personnel after the preparation of a Credit Slip after the Credit Slip has been prepared.
Copies of the Credit Slip, and when applicable, the stored data, must contain at least the following information:
The denominations of the value chips, coins, and plaques being returned to the chip bank;
The total amount of each denomination of value chips, coins and plaques being returned;
The total amount of all denominations of value chips, coins, and plaques being returned;
The game and table number from which the value chips, coins, and plaques are being returned;
The date and shift during which the removal of value chips, coins, and plaques occurs; and
The signature of the preparer or, if electronically prepared, the identification code of the preparer.
When the Credit Slip is prepare, the time of its preparation shall be recorded on the drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip.

After the Credit Slip has been prepared by the chip bank cashier or has been printed in the chip bank as a result of the information being inputted electronically by a pit clerk or floorperson or above, the security department employee shall transport the drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip directly to the gaming table.

The dealer or boxperson shall compare the value chips, coins and plaques to be removed from the table inventory container with the drop box copy of the Credit Slip and the Credit Request Slip, if applicable, and shall sign the Credit Slip.

Signatures on the drop box, acknowledgement and chip bank copies of a Credit Slip attesting to the accuracy of the information contained on the Credit Slip shall be required of the specified employees at the specified times:
The chip bank cashier, upon receipt of the value chips from the table;

The dealer or boxperson assigned to the gaming table, upon removal of the value chips, coins and plaques from the table inventory container and verification of the Credit Slip and Credit Request Slip, if applicable;

The floorperson assigned to the gaming table, upon observing the removal of the value chips, coins and plaques from the table inventory container and the verification of the Credit Slip and Credit Request Slip, if applicable; and
The security department employee, upon receipt of the value chips, coins and plaques from the gaming table.
After meeting the signature requirements required under §P of this regulation:
The security department employee shall transport the value chips, coins and plaques directly to the chip bank along with the acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip;
The dealer or boxperson shall place the drop box copy of the Credit Slip on the gaming table in view of the facility’s surveillance system;
Upon receipt of the value chips, coins and plaques from the security department employee, the chip bank cashier shall:
Compare the chip bank copy of the Credit Request Slip with the Credit Slip; or
If the credit request is electronically generated in accordance with §G of this regulation, the chip bank cashier shall compare the Credit Slip with the electronically generated credit request; and
The chip bank cashier shall attest to the accuracy of the credit by signing the acknowledgement and chip bank copies of the Credit Slip.

After transporting the acknowledgement copy of the Credit Slip back to the gaming table from which the value chips, coins and plaques were removed, the security department employee shall observe the immediate placement by the dealer or boxperson of the drop box copy of the Credit Request Slip, if applicable, and the drop box and acknowledgement copies of the Credit Slip into the drop box.
The chip bank copies of the Credit Request Slip, if applicable, and Credit Slip shall be maintained together by the chip bank cashier until forwarded to the accounting department.

All parts of voided Credit Slips, chip bank copies of Credit Request Slips, if applicable, and the chip bank copies of the Credit Slips that are maintained and controlled in conformity with §Q of this regulation, shall be forwarded by a chip bank cashier to the accounting department for agreement, on a daily basis, with:
The drop box copies of the Credit Request Slips, if applicable, and the drop box and acknowledgement copies of the Credit Slips removed from the drop box on the gaming table; and
The electronically stored data and accounting copies of Credit Slips, if applicable.

para.text: A skills-based amusement device is not legally operated if:
Unless authorized under COMAR 36.08.03, the merchandise, prizes, tickets, tokens, or other objects awarded exceeds the minimal value;
The device awards a cash prize or a prize that is readily convertible to cash;
The outcome of the game is based on a preponderance of chance;
The ability of any player to succeed at the game is impacted by the number or ratio of prior wins to prior losses of that device;
The outcome of the game can be controlled by a source other than the player of the game;
The success of any player is or may be determined by a chance event which cannot be altered by player actions;
The ability of a player to succeed at the game is impacted by game features not visible or known to a reasonable player; or
The ability of a player to succeed at the game is impacted by the exercise of skill that no reasonable player could exercise.
The individual merchandise, prizes, tickets, tokens, or other objects that are awarded by a skill-based amusement device may:
Have a wholesale value that does not exceed the minimal value; and
Be accumulated and exchanged for non-cash merchandise or prizes that do not exceed $599.
If a registered skills-based device owner offers a device described in §A of this regulation, the Commission may take enforcement action as provided in Regulation .03 of this chapter.

section.heading: Class A-1 Sports Wagering Facility License.

para.text: The Commission shall issue a Class A-1 Sports Wagering Facility License to an applicant that meets all applicable licensing requirements under State Government Article, Title 9, Subtitle 1E, Annotated Code of Maryland, and the Commissions regulations.
A Class A-1 Sports Wagering Facility License may only be issued to:
A video lottery operator with more than 1,000 video lottery terminals;
For a stadium in Prince Georges County that is primarily used for professional football (NFL), a license applicant that is:
The owner; or
The designee if an applicant designated an entity to hold the license;
The owner, or the designee of the owner, of a professional football franchise that is a lessee of a stadium in Baltimore City;
The owner, or the designee of the owner, of a professional major league baseball franchise that is a lessee of a stadium in Baltimore City;
The owner, or the designee of the owner, of a professional hockey league franchise, that is a lessee of a stadium in the State;
The owner, or the designee of the owner, of a professional basketball association franchise, that is a lessee of a stadium in the State; or
The owner, or the designee of the owner, of a professional soccer league franchise, that is a lessee of a stadium in the State.
The non-refundable application fee for a Class A-1 sports wagering facility is $2,000,000.
The performance bond for a Class A-1 sports wagering facility license is:
$2,000,000 for annual sports wagering revenue of $0 to $2,000,000;
$4,000,000 for annual sports wagering revenue of $2,000,001 to $4,000,000; and
$6,000,000 $4,000,000 for annual sports wagering revenue above $4,000,000.
Designee Designation. If an applicant that is an owner identified under §B(2)(7) of this regulation identifies a designee, the owner shall:

Complete and execute an Affidavit of Owner of a Professional Sports Franchise or Stadium Supporting the Owners Designee to be the Applicant for, and Holder of, a Sports Wagering Facility License that:
Identifies the designee;

Acknowledges that the sports wagering law authorizes the Commission to issue a Class A-1 sports wagering facility license to an owner of a specified professional sports franchise or stadium, or to the owners designee, if the applicant meets all applicable requirements;
Understands, acknowledges, and certifies that the designee is required to meet all applicable requirements for:
Qualification by the Commission;
License award by SWARC;
License issuance by the Commission; and
Continuing obligations for the term of the license;

Understands, acknowledges, and certifies that the owner is prohibited from controlling or influencing the designee in the designees capacity as an applicant for or holder of a sports wagering facility license; and

Understands, acknowledges, and certifies that the owner is prohibited from receiving from the designee any interest, percentage, or share of handle, proceeds, or profit, that is based on sports wagering; and
Submit to the Commission documentation establishing that it has no control or influence over an owners designee.
Designee Responsibilities.

The designee shall complete and execute an Affidavit of Designee of an Owner of a Professional Sports Franchise or Stadium Supporting the Owners Designee to be the Applicant for, and Holder of, a Sports Wagering Facility License that meets the same requirements in §E(1) of this regulation.
The designee shall submit to the Commission documentation that the owner has no control or influence over it.
Designee Substitution.
If an owner seeks to replace the named designee, the owner and the designee shall submit to the Commission a request to replace the designee as the holder of the license.
The new designee shall submit to the Commission an application for a sports wagering facility license.

The Commission may transfer the sports wagering facility license to the new designee after the Commission determines the new designee meets all applicable requirements for a sports wagering facility license, and issues the license.

para.text: Definitions.
In this regulation, the following terms have the meaning indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Advertisement” means any material that is:
Disseminated to the public through broadcasting, publication, mail, or any other means; and
Intended to encourage sports wagering.
“Billboard advertisement” means a roadside sign, aviation banner, or event banner that is intended to encourage sports wagering.
“Gambling assistance message” means a phrase approved by the Commission to encourage responsible play.
“Printed advertisement” means an advertisement that appears in or on a sign, direct mailing, poster, brochure or other written material and is intended to encourage sports wagering.
“Online advertisement” means an advertisement that appears on a website, an application in social media, or otherwise on the internet that is intended to encourage sports wagering.
“Responsible gambling awareness materials” means a sticker, a brochure, a wallet card, or other material that conveys only problem gambling resource information.
“Underage warning message” means the phrase: No bettor under the age of 21 is permitted to participate in sports wagering.
A sports wagering licensee shall:
Post signage approved by the Commission that prominently bears the gambling assistance message and the underage warning message at each customer entrance;
Include banners or other notifications on the sports wagering websites that bear the gambling assistance message and the underage warning message;
Include the gambling assistance message on an advertisement that is intended to encourage sports wagering;
Ensure that a printed advertisement bears the gambling assistance message and meets requirements of COMAR 36.10.13.43 and 36.03.03.08;
Ensure that a billboard bearing a printed advertisement bears the gambling assistance message and meets requirements of COMAR 36.10.13.43 and 36.03.03.08;
Ensure that a radio, television, video, online, or social media advertisement bears the gambling assistance message and meets requirements of COMAR 36.10.13.43 and 36.03.03.08;
Ensure that the gambling assistance message is printed on a paper product that is associated with bettor consumption of food or beverage if the paper product is:
Special ordered; and
Branded with the sports wagering licensees logo;
Ensure that the gambling assistance message is printed on a sports wagering ticket or sports wagering voucher;
Place in the sports wagering facility and sports wagering platform responsible gambling awareness information according to its responsible gaming plan required under COMAR 36.10.10.02;
Ensure that an advertisement for sports wagering, video lottery terminal, or table game play does not include language that suggests an outcome is guaranteed or without risk; and
Ensure that advertisements for sports wagering includes a gambling assistance message that complies:
For signs, direct mail materials, posters, and other printed advertisements the font height of the gambling assistance message shall be the greater of:
The same size as the majority of the text in the advertisement; or
Three percent of the height or width, whichever is greater, of the advertisement;
For billboards, the font height of the gambling assistance message shall be at least 5 percent of the height or width, whichever is greater, of the face of the billboard;
For video and television advertisements:
The gambling assistance message shall be visible for the entire duration of the advertisement, with a font size at least 3 percent of the image height or width;

The gambling assistance message shall appear from the first instance sports wagering or a licensed operator is displayed or mentioned for not less than 3 seconds, and again on a final screen for the final 3 seconds of the advertisement;
The font height shall be at least 3 percent of the height or width, whichever is greater, of the image during the advertisement; or
The font height shall be at least 8 percent of the height or width, whichever is greater, of the image on the final screen; and
For websites and social media:
The gambling assistance message shall be posted on each webpage or profile page, and on any gaming-related advertisement posted on those pages;
The font height of the gambling assistance message shall be at least the same size as the majority of the text used on the webpage or profile page; and
For advertisements posted on the webpage or profile page, the font height shall comply with the requirements in §B(11)(a) of this regulation.

section.heading: Collection of Cash Storage and Drop Boxes.

para.text: At least 30 days before sports wagering operations are to commence or other time period approved by the Commission, a sports wagering licensee shall submit to the Commission in writing a drop schedule setting forth specific pick-up days and times for collection of cash storage drop boxes, including:
Specifications as to what areas of the sports wagering facility will be covered on each pick-up day; and
Specific transportation routes to be utilized from the sports wagering facility floor to the count room on each pick-up day.
A sports wagering licensee shall notify the Commission:
In writing, of a permanent change in the drop schedule including a pick-up day or time, area of the floor to be dropped, or transportation route; and
Prior to any temporary deviation from the drop schedule.
A sports wagering licensee shall make readily available to the Commission:
An access control matrix indicating which employee job descriptions are authorized to participate in the cash storage drop box collection process; and
A list of employees, with license numbers, who are authorized to participate in the cash storage drop box collection process.

A sports wagering licensee shall develop and include in the internal controls submitted to and approved by the Commission under Regulation .04 of this chapter procedures addressing the collection of cash storage drop boxes.
A sports wagering licensees internal controls shall:
Detail the actual procedures to be performed and documentation to be generated by drop team employees collecting cash storage drop boxes:
In accordance with the drop schedule; and
On an emergency basis; and
Require:
Cash storage drop boxes to be transported directly to, and secured in:
The count room; or
A trolley storage area approved by the Commission under COMAR 36.10.15.04B;
Except as provided in §F of this regulation, the cash storage drop box collection process to involve the participation of at least three employees, at least one of whom is an employee of the:
Security department or other department approved by the Commission; and
Accounting department;

Prior to the movement of a trolley containing cash storage boxes from the sports wagering facility floor into the count room, an accounting department supervisor to verify that the number of cash storage boxes being transported from the facility floor equals the number of cash storage boxes scheduled for collection that day; and
Prior to changing the type of a kiosk, or removing a kiosk from the sports wagering facility floor, that an emergency drop shall be conducted.
The Commission may allow the holder of a Class B-2 sports wagering license to perform the cash storage drop box collection process with at least two employees, one of whom is an employee of the:
Security department or other department approved by the Commission; and
Accounting department.
A sports wagering licensee shall transport cash storage drop boxes in an enclosed trolley secured by one lock that has a key which is controlled by the security department.
A sports wagering licensee shall store cash storage drop boxes not attached to a bill validator, including emergency cash storage drop boxes that are not actively in use:
In the count room in an area approved by the Commission;
In a trolley storage area approved by the Commission under COMAR 36.10.15.04B; or
In another location at the sports facility approved by the Commission.
The cabinet or trolley used for storage under §G of this regulation shall be secured by one lock that has a key which is controlled by the security department in:
A manual key box; or
An automated key tracking system.

Immediately prior to the commencement of the count process, the security department may issue its key to the storage cabinet or trolley required under §G of this regulation to a count room supervisor for the purpose of allowing count room personnel to gain access to the cash storage drop boxes to be counted.
A trolley storage area utilized to store cash storage drop boxes prior to the count process shall meet the design standards for a count room under Regulation .35 of this chapter.
Except for security officers, the drop and count team members assigned to the collection of cash storage drop boxes shall wear a one-piece, pocketless jumpsuit.

para.text: A sports wagering licensee shall maintain a reserve in cash, cash equivalents, irrevocable letter of credit, surety bond set forth in §F of this regulation, or a combination thereof in an amount approved by the Commission to cover the outstanding liability of the sports wagering licensee to bettors.

Except for funds held in the reserve account that exceed the licensee’s reserve requirement specified in §C of this regulation, a sports wagering licensee may not remove, release, or withdraw funds from its reserve without the written approval of the Commission..
The amount in the reserve shall be at least $500,000 and equal or exceed the aggregate sum of:
The total amount of funds to cover the potential liability for all wagers accepted by the sports wagering licensee on sporting events with outcomes that have not been determined; and
Money owed but unpaid by the sports wagering licensee to bettors on winning wagers.
A sports wagering licensee shall ensure that the reserve is held:
By a financial institution insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and licensed to transact business in the State; or
For sports wagering facility licensee, in cash held on facility premises.
A sports wagering licensee shall:
Calculate its reserve requirements each day; and
If the sports wagering licensee determines its reserve is insufficient to cover the requirement of this regulation, notify the Commission in writing:
Within 24 hours of the deficiency; and
The steps to be taken to remedy the deficiency.

A sports wagering licensee may satisfy the reserve requirements in this regulation if the licensee adds sufficient funds to cover the calculated requirement prior to the end of the following business day.
Surety Bonds.

The sports wagering licensee may obtain a single surety bond or multiple bonds to be used as a cash reserve to pay the current outstanding liability of all winnings and awards offered to a winning bettor as set forth in this regulation and COMAR 36.10.13.40.
A surety bond shall:
Be in a form approved by the Agency and:
Identify the sports wagering licensee or applicant as the principal of the surety bond;
Indicate what license type and class the sports wagering licensee or applicant offers or intends to offer in the State of Maryland;
Provide that the surety bond is protected against claims by creditors of the principal, including the patrons for whose benefit and protection the reserve account is established;
Allow the Director of the Agency or the Director’s designee to make demand upon the surety for the payment of valid winning amounts not paid by the principal;
Require the sports wagering licensee to provide an updated listing of winning wagers that form the basis of the reserve to the Agency within 72 hours;

Include a statement that within 24 hours of receiving the updated listing of winning wagers that form the basis of the reserve that the Agency will commence review of the list of outstanding patron accounts;
Include a statement that within 10 business days of the demand made by the Director or the Director’s designee the processing of the payment will commence;
Include a statement that within 60 days of the demand made by the Director or the Director’s designee issuance of the payment shall occur; and
Include a statement that if the surety provides written notice of withdrawal, the withdrawal is not effective until 60 days have elapsed after receipt of the notice.
Be by and between the sports wagering licensee or applicant and the surety company;
Identify the Agency as the obligee;
Specify that it guarantees the portion of the sports wagering licensee’s reserve that is not covered by a cash reserve; and
Specify that the surety bond is valid for at least the 5-year license term.
The sports wagering licensee or applicant shall provide the original surety bond to the Agency.

Agency staff may not issue a sports wagering license unless the applicant or awardee has provided Agency staff with a copy of any surety bond that comprises any portion of the reserve the sports wagering licensee is required to hold.

para.text: A sports wagering licensee shall keep its geolocation system up to date by integrating current solutions in real time that can detect the use of:
Remote desktop software;
Rootkits;
Virtualization; and
Any other programs identified by the Commission as having the ability to circumvent geolocation measures.
The sports wagering licensee shall continually review the integrity of the geolocation system to ensure that the system detects and mitigates existing and emerging location fraud risks.
A sports wagering platform shall utilize a geolocation system to:
Reasonably detect the physical location of an individual attempting to access the sports wagering platform and place a sports wager; and
Monitor and block attempts to place an online sports wager when an individual is not within the State.
The geolocation system required by §C of this regulation shall:
Ensure that a bettor:
Is located within the State when placing a sports wager; and
Can dynamically monitor the bettors location; and
Block unauthorized attempts to place a sports wager when an individual is not within the State.
A sports wagering licensee shall:
Prior to commencing operations, and annually thereafter, ensure:
Its geolocation system is tested by a Commission approved testing laboratory; and
A report on the annual testing is submitted to the Commission no later than 120 days after the end of the licensee’s fiscal year;
Ensure that testing of the geolocation system includes:
Attempts to place wagers at locations from outside of the State from multiple locations within varying distances from the State border;
Attempts to place wagers at locations within the State from multiple locations within varying distances from the State border; and
Attempts to place wagers in which the tester crosses the State border;
For all geolocation testing, ensure that the frequency of geolocation check is documented and tested;
Ensure that testing confirms that a geolocation check occurs immediately upon a change of IP address;
Ensure that testing includes static and mobile connections; and
Ensure that testing is completed on all device types using the most prevalent versions available.
A sports wagering licensee shall grant the Commission read only access to its geolocation system as required by the Commission.
In addition to the requirements in this regulation, the Commission may require a sports wagering licensee to implement other geolocation features.
A sports wagering licensee shall:

Implement, maintain, regularly review and revise, and comply with a comprehensive information security system that reasonably protects the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of a bettor’s personally identifiable information; and
Ensure that the security system set forth in §A(1) of this regulation includes administrative, technical, and physical safeguards which:
Are appropriate to the size, complexity, nature, and scope of the operations; and
Protect the personal information owned, licensed, maintained, handled, or otherwise in the possession of the sports wagering licensee.
A sports wagering licensee shall:

Within 90 days of commencing operations, and annually thereafter, conduct a vulnerability assessment, penetration testing, and operational security control review against ISO 27001 standard, or other similar standards such as CIS or NIST CSF;
Perform vulnerability assessments and penetration testing of the sports wagering platform at multiple layers, including:
Internal and external network;
Mobile and web application;
Database;
Firewall;
If applicable, wireless; and
Any additional security testing that the Commission requires;
Ensure that a Commission approved third party described in Regulation .02B of this chapter conducts the testing required in §B(1) and (2) of this regulation;
Ensure that the annual reporting requirement required in §B(1)—(3) of this regulation is submitted to the Commission no later than 120 days after the end of the licensee’s fiscal year;
Perform internal quarterly vulnerability scans; and
Submit to the Commission documentation of the scan results and the actions taken to resolve identified vulnerabilities.
A sports wagering licensee shall submit to the Commission the assessment report issued by the third party and the licensee’s report.
The combined reports in §C of this regulation shall:
Provide details for all vulnerabilities identified;
Assess the adequacy and effectiveness of the sports wagering licensee’s information technology security controls and system configurations; and
Provide recommendations for eliminating each material weakness or significant deficiency identified.
A sports wagering licensee shall evaluate all identified vulnerabilities for potential adverse effect on security and integrity and:
Remediate the vulnerability no later than 90 days following the earlier of vulnerability’s identification or public disclosure; or
Document why remediation action is unnecessary or unsuitable.
